name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَدَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ إِذْ يَحْكُمَانِ فِى ٱلْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ ٱلْقَوْمِ وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شَـٰهِدِينَ (٧٨)
```

```english
And [remember] David and Solomon when they gave judgement concerning the tillage when the sheep of some people strayed into it by night, and We were witness to their judgement. (78)
```

```arabic
فَفَهَّمْنَـٰهَا سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ وَكُلًّا ءَاتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَسَخَّرْنَا مَعَ دَاوُۥدَ ٱلْجِبَالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۚ وَكُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ (٧٩)
```

```english
We gave its understanding to Solomon, and to each We gave judgement and knowledge. We disposed the mountains and the birds to glorify [Us] with David, and We have been the doer [of these things]. (79)
```

```arabic
وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَّكُمْ لِتُحْصِنَكُم مِّنۢ بَأْسِكُمْ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُمْ شَـٰكِرُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
We taught him the making of coats of mail for you, to protect you from your [own] violence. Will you then be grateful? (80)
```

```arabic
وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ عَاصِفَةً تَجْرِى بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۚ وَكُنَّا بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَـٰلِمِينَ (٨١)
```

```english
And [We disposed] for Solomon the tempestuous wind which blew by his command toward the land which We have blessed, and We have knowledge of all things. (81)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ مَن يَغُوصُونَ لَهُۥ وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ (٨٢)
```

```english
Among the devils were some who dived for him and performed tasks other than that, and We were watchful over them. (82)
```

```arabic
۞ وَأَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلضُّرُّ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
And [remember] Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘Indeed distress has befallen me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful.’ (83)
```

```arabic
فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ فَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِۦ مِن ضُرٍّ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَهْلَهُۥ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰبِدِينَ (٨٤)
```

```english
So We answered his prayer and removed his distress, and We gave him [back] his family along with others like them, as a mercy from Us and an admonition for the devout. (84)
```

```arabic
وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِدْرِيسَ وَذَا ٱلْكِفْلِ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
And [remember] Ishmael, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl—each of them was among the patient. (85)
```

```arabic
وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٦)
```

```english
We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed, they were among the righteous. (86)
```

```arabic
وَذَا ٱلنُّونِ إِذ ذَّهَبَ مُغَـٰضِبًا فَظَنَّ أَن لَّن نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ فَنَادَىٰ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ أَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنتَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ إِنِّى كُنتُ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking that We would not put him to hardship. Then he cried out in the darkness, ‘There is no god except You! You are immaculate! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!’ (87)
```

```arabic
فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُـۨجِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨٨)
```

```english
So We answered his prayer and delivered him from the agony; and thus do We deliver the faithful. (88)
```

```arabic
وَزَكَرِيَّآ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥ رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْنِى فَرْدًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (٨٩)
```

```english
And [remember] Zechariah, when he cried out to his Lord, ‘My Lord! Do not leave me without an heir, and You are the best of inheritors.’ (89)
```

```arabic
فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ وَأَصْلَحْنَا لَهُۥ زَوْجَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَيَدْعُونَنَا رَغَبًا وَرَهَبًا ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ لَنَا خَـٰشِعِينَ (٩٠)
```

```english
So We answered his prayer, and gave him John, and cured for him his wife [of infertility]. Indeed, they were active in [performing] good works, and they would supplicate Us with eagerness and awe and were humble before Us. (90)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّتِىٓ أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهَا مِن رُّوحِنَا وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا وَٱبْنَهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩١)
```

```english
And [remember] her who guarded her chastity, so We breathed into her Our spirit, and made her and her son a sign for all the nations. (91)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses mention another group of prophets: David, Solomon, Job, Ishmael, Idris, Dhul-Kifl, Dhul-Nun, Zechariah, John, and Jesus (peace be upon them). In mentioning them, the chronological order was not observed, nor was there a transition from the later to the earlier as in the preceding verses. The Exalted has pointed toward some of His magnificent favors upon some of them, while sufficing with the mere mention of the names of others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَداوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ الْقَوْمِ** — "And David and Solomon, when they gave judgment concerning the tillage, when the sheep of some people strayed into it by night" (21:78) until His saying: **حُكْماً وَعِلْماً** — "judgment and knowledge" (21:79). The tillage (الحرث) refers to crops, and it is also used for vineyards. Straying (النفش) refers to the grazing of livestock at night. In *al-Majma‘*, it is stated: *al-Nafsh* (النفش), with either a fatha or sukun on the *fa*, means the spreading of camels and sheep at night to graze without a shepherd. End quote.

His saying: **وَداوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ** — "And David and Solomon, when they gave judgment concerning the tillage, when [the sheep] strayed into it." The context indicates that this was a single specific incident whose judgment was brought before David because he was the king and judge over the Children of Israel.

Allah had appointed him as a vicegerent (خليفة) — *a representative or deputy of Allah on earth* — as He said: **يا داوُدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ** — "O David! Indeed We have made you a vicegerent on the earth. So judge between the people with justice" (38:26). Thus, if Solomon intervened in the judgment of the incident, it was by David's permission and for some wisdom, perhaps to demonstrate Solomon's worthiness for the vicegerency after David.

It is well known that there is no meaning to the judgment of two judges in a single personal incident if each is independent in judgment and authority. From here, it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: **إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ** (when they gave judgment) is "when they were deliberating or consulting regarding the judgment," not the issuance of a binding verdict. This is strongly supported by the expression **إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ** (when they were judging) in the form of a present-tense narrative of a past state (حكاية الحال الماضية), as if they had begun the process of judging gradually, which had not yet been completed and would only conclude with a single binding judgment. Otherwise, it would have been more apparent to say: "when they judged" (إذ حكما).

This is also supported by His saying: **وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شاهِدِينَ** — "and We were witnesses to their judgment" (21:78). The apparent meaning is that the pronoun in "their judgment" (لحكمهم) refers to the prophets, as it is repeated in the speech of the Exalted that He gave them "judgment" (الحُكم). It is not as some have said, that the pronoun refers to David, Solomon, and the parties to the dispute, for there is no basis at all for attributing the judgment to the parties involved. Thus, the judgment was a single judgment, which is the judgment of the prophets. It appears that it concerned the liability of the owner of the sheep for the property destroyed by his sheep.

The judgment was thus a single judgment, though they differed in the manner of its practical implementation. Had the difference been in the essence of the judgment, the issuance of two judgments from them would have to be assumed in one of two ways: either both judgments were the actual judgment of Allah (حكماً واقعياً), with one—Solomon's—abrogating the other—David's—based on the saying of the Exalted: **فَفَهَّمْناها سُلَيْمانَ** — "We gave its understanding to Solomon" (21:79); or both judgments were based on their independent legal reasoning (اجتهاد) — *the process of deriving legal rulings through intellectual effort* — in the sense of a speculative opinion (الرأي الظني) while being ignorant of the actual divine judgment, and Allah then validated Solomon's reasoning, making it the final judgment.

As for the first possibility—that Solomon's judgment abrogated David's—there should be no doubt that the apparent meaning of the verses does not support it. The abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ) are mutually exclusive. If their judgments were of the nature of abrogation and were mutually exclusive, it would have been said: "We were witnesses to their [dual] judgment" (لحكمهما) to indicate multiplicity and variance. It would not have said: **وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شاهِدِينَ** (and We were witnesses to their judgment), which suggests the unity of the judgment. Furthermore, Allah being a "witness" to it implies their protection from error. Had David judged in the incident with an abrogated ruling, he would have been in error. Moreover, His saying: **وَكُلًّا آتَيْنا حُكْماً وَعِلْماً** — "and to each We gave judgment and knowledge" (21:79) does not suit this, as it implies endorsement and is clearly intended as praise.

As for the second possibility—that both judgments were based on independent reasoning (اجتهاد) while being ignorant of Allah's actual judgment—it is even further from the truth than the first. This is because the Exalted says: **فَفَهَّمْناها سُلَيْمانَ** (We gave its understanding to Solomon), which refers to knowledge of Allah's actual judgment.

How could this apply to a speculative opinion (الرأي الظني) insofar as it is merely speculative? Furthermore, He says: **وَكُلًّا آتَيْنا حُكْماً وَعِلْماً** (and to each We gave judgment and knowledge), which confirms that what David judged with was also a judgment based on knowledge (علمياً), not speculation (ظنياً). If His saying: "and to each We gave judgment and knowledge" did not include David's judgment in this incident, there would be no reason to mention the sentence in this context.

Moreover, as you have heard, His saying: **وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شاهِدِينَ** (and We were witnesses to their judgment) is not devoid of an indication—indeed, a proof—that the judgment was one and protected from error. Thus, nothing remains but that their judgment was one in itself, differing only in the manner of its implementation, and Solomon's judgment was more appropriate and gentler.

Narrations from both the Shi‘a and Sunni paths have been transmitted, the gist of which is that David judged that the owner of the tillage should receive the necks (the physical bodies) of the sheep, while Solomon judged that he should receive their benefits for that year, such as milk, wool, and offspring.

Perhaps the judgment was the liability for what the sheep had destroyed of the tillage, and this was equal to the value of the sheep's bodies. Thus, David judged that their bodies be given to the owner of the tillage. Solomon judged with what was gentler: that the owner should recover what was destroyed of his property from the benefits of the sheep for that year. Usually, the benefits obtained from sheep each year are equivalent in value to the value of the animal itself.

So His saying: **وَداوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ** means "and remember David and Solomon"; **إِذْ** "when"; **يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ** "they were giving judgment concerning the tillage"; **إِذْ** "when"; **نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ الْقَوْمِ** "the sheep of the people strayed into it at night and destroyed it"; **وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ** "and We were [witnesses] to their judgment," meaning the judgment of the prophets. It has been said that the pronoun refers to David, Solomon, and the one in whose favor the judgment was made, but you have seen the flaw in this. It has also been said that the pronoun is for David and Solomon because two can be treated as a plural, but this is as you see [weak]. **شاهِدِينَ** "witnesses," meaning present, seeing and hearing, and making them aware of the correct way therein. **فَفَهَّمْناها** "We gave its understanding," meaning the governance and the case, **سُلَيْمانَ وَكُلًّا** "to Solomon, and to each" of David and Solomon **آتَيْنا حُكْماً وَعِلْماً** "We gave judgment and knowledge." It is sometimes said that the beginning of the verse is estimated as: "And We gave David and Solomon judgment and knowledge when they were judging..." etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَسَخَّرْنا مَعَ داوُدَ الْجِبالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَالطَّيْرَ وَكُنَّا فاعِلِينَ** — "And We disposed the mountains to celebrate Our glory with David, and the birds [as well], and We have been the doers [of such things]" (21:79). Subjugation (التسخير) is the making of a thing subservient such that its action, as it is, serves the purposes of the subjugator (المسخِّر). This is different from compulsion (الإجبار), coercion (الإكراه), or constraint (القسر), for in those cases, the agent acts outside the requirement of its choice or nature. In contrast, the subjugated agent (المسخَّر) acts according to the requirement of its nature and choice. For example, a person burning wood with fire is a subjugated act of the fire, yet it is not constrained. Similarly, the work of a hired laborer for his employer is a subjugated act of the laborer, yet he is neither compelled nor coerced.

From here, it becomes clear that the meaning of subjugating the mountains and birds with David to glorify Allah with him is that they possess a glorification (تسبيح) — *the declaration of Allah's purity from all defects* — in themselves, and their subjugation was that they should glorify with David in harmony with his glorification. Thus, His saying: **يُسَبِّحْنَ** (celebrating glory) is an explanation of His saying: **وَسَخَّرْنا مَعَ داوُدَ** (and We disposed with David), and His saying: **وَالطَّيْرَ** (and the birds) is conjoined to "the mountains."

His saying: **وَكُنَّا فاعِلِينَ** (and We have been the doers) means that such gifts and favors were part of Our sunnah (سنتنا) — *the established way or practice of Allah* — and what We bestowed upon them was not an unprecedented thing from Us.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعَلَّمْناهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَكُمْ لِتُحْصِنَكُمْ مِنْ بَأْسِكُمْ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ شاكِرُونَ** — "We taught him the making of coats of mail for you, to protect you from your [mutual] violence. Will you then be grateful?" (21:80). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: *al-Labus* (اللبوس) is a name for all weaponry among the Arabs... until he said: and it is said it refers to the coat of mail. End quote. In *al-Mufradat*: and the saying of the Exalted: **صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَكُمْ** refers to the coat of mail.

Violence (البأس) refers to the intensity of combat, and it seems the meaning in the verse is the intensity of the impact of weapons. The pronoun in **وَعَلَّمْناهُ** (and We taught him) refers to David, as He said in another place: **وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ** — "and We made iron supple for him" (34:10). The meaning is: We taught David the craft of your coats of mail—meaning We taught him how to make coats of mail for you to protect you and prevent the intensity of the impact of weapons. His saying: **فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ شاكِرُونَ** (Will you then be grateful?) is an affirmation of the duty of gratitude.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ عاصِفَةً تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ** — "And [We disposed] for Solomon the tempestuous wind which blew by his command..." (21:81) etc. This is conjoined to His saying "with David," meaning: and We subjugated for Solomon the wind, tempestuous (عاصفة), meaning blowing strongly, the wind blowing by his command to the land which We had blessed, which is the land of Sham (Greater Syria) where Solomon used to reside. "And We have knowledge of all things."

The mention of subjugating the wind as "tempestuous" (عاصفة), even though the wind was subjugated to him in both its strong and gentle states—as He said: **رُخاءً حَيْثُ أَصابَ** — "blowing gently wherever he intended" (38:36)—is because the subjugation of the wind when it is tempestuous is more wondrous and more indicative of power.

It has been said: because of the common knowledge that he (peace be upon him) resided in that land, its blowing by his command *from* there was not mentioned, and the mention was restricted to its blowing *to* it, which is more apparent in expressing favor. End quote. It is possible that the meaning is its blowing by his command toward it so that it might carry him from it to wherever he wished, not its blowing toward it only to return him to it and set him down there after having carried him. Based on this, the speech includes both departing from it and returning to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنَ الشَّياطِينِ مَنْ يَغُوصُونَ لَهُ وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلاً دُونَ ذلِكَ وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حافِظِينَ** — "And of the devils [We disposed] those who dived for him and performed other tasks besides that, and We were watchful over them" (21:82). The diving was to extract the treasures of the sea, such as pearls and others. The "other tasks besides that" refers to what He mentioned in His saying: **يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَتَماثِيلَ وَجِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ وَقُدُورٍ راسِياتٍ** — "They made for him whatever he wished of edifices, statues, basins [large] as water troughs, and fixed cauldrons" (34:13). The "watchfulness over the devils" refers to keeping them in his service and preventing them from fleeing, refusing, or causing corruption for him. The meaning is clear, and the stories of David and Solomon (peace be upon them) will come in Surah Saba, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَيُّوبَ إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَأَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ** — "And [remember] Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘Indeed distress has befallen me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful’" (21:83). Distress (الضُّرُّ), with a damma, refers specifically to what touches the soul of harm, such as illness, emaciation, and the like; with a fatha (الضَّرُّ), it is more general.

Affliction had encompassed him (peace be upon him); his wealth was gone, his children died, and he was afflicted in his body with a severe illness for a long period. Then he called upon Allah and complained of his state to Him, so Allah answered him and saved him from his illness and restored to him his wealth and children, and the like of them with them. This is His saying in the following verse: **فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ فَكَشَفْنا ما بِهِ مِنْ ضُرٍّ** — "So We answered him and removed the distress that was upon him" (21:84), meaning We saved him from his illness and healed him. **وَآتَيْناهُ أَهْلَهُ** "and We gave him his family," meaning those of his children who had died, **وَمِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَذِكْرى لِلْعابِدِينَ** "and the like of them with them, as a mercy from Us and an admonition for the devout" (21:84), so that they may remember and know that Allah afflicts His friends as a trial from Him, then He gives them their reward, and He does not waste the reward of the virtuous.

The story of Job (peace be upon him) will come in Surah Sad, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِسْماعِيلَ وَإِدْرِيسَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ** — "And Ishmael, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl..." (21:85) etc. As for Idris (peace be upon him), his story has preceded in Surah Maryam. As for Ishmael, his story will come in Surah al-Saffat. The story of Dhul-Kifl will come in Surah Sad, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ** — "And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking that We would not put him to hardship..." (21:87) etc.

*Al-Nun* (النون) is the fish, and *Dhu al-Nun* (the Man of the Fish) is the Prophet Jonah (Yunus), son of Amittai (Matta), the companion of the fish, who was sent to the people of Nineveh. He called them, but they did not believe, so he asked Allah to punish them. When the punishment drew near them, they repented and believed, so Allah removed it from them. Jonah had already departed from them, so Allah afflicted him by having a fish swallow him. He called out to Him from its belly, so He removed the distress from him and sent him a second time to his people.

His saying: **وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ** means: and remember the Man of the Fish when he left in a rage—meaning at his people because they did not believe in him—and he thought that We would not "decree" (نقدر) against him, meaning We would not restrict him, as in "he whose provision is restricted (قُدِرَ عليه)" (65:7).

It is possible that His saying: **إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ** is mentioned by way of representation (تمثيل); that is, his departure and separation from his people was like the departure of one who was enraged at his master, thinking that his master would not be able to reach him and that he would escape him by distancing himself, such that the master would have no power to discipline him. As for him (peace be upon him) being truly enraged at his Lord or genuinely thinking that Allah had no power over him, this is something from which the station of the noble prophets is certainly far removed, for they are protected by the protection (عصمة) — *the divine safeguarding from sin and error* — of Allah.

His saying: **فَنادى فِي الظُّلُماتِ** — "Then he cried out in the darknesses" (21:87) etc. In this, there is a concise deletion (إيجاز بالحذف), and the speech is branched upon it. The estimation is: "So Allah afflicted him with the fish, and it swallowed him, and he called out to his Lord from its belly." It appears that the "darknesses" (الظلمات), as has been said, refer to the darkness of the sea, the darkness of the fish's belly, and the darkness of the night.

His saying: **أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ** — "There is no god but You! Immaculate are You!" (21:87). This is a disavowal (تبرؤ) by him (peace be upon him) of what his departure and separation from his people without being commanded had represented. For that departure represented—even if he did not intend it or do it deliberately—that there was a place of return one could go to other than his Lord; so he disavowed that by his saying: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ** (There is no god but You). It also represented that it was permissible to object to His act and be enraged by it, and that it was possible for someone to escape Him and leave the realm of His power; so he disavowed that by declaring His transcendence (تنزيهه) with his saying: **سُبْحانَكَ** (Immaculate are You).

His saying: **إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "Indeed I have been among the wrongdoers" (21:87). This is an admission of wrongdoing (الظلم) insofar as he performed an act that represented wrongdoing, even if it was not wrongdoing in itself, nor did he (peace be upon him) intend wrongdoing or disobedience by it. However, this was a disciplining from Him, the Exalted, and a cultivation (تربية) of His prophet, so that he might tread the carpet of proximity with a step that is free in its gait from even the representation of wrongdoing, let alone wrongdoing itself.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَنَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ وَكَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "So We answered him and delivered him from the agony, and thus do We deliver the faithful" (21:88). Although he (peace be upon him) did not explicitly state any request or supplication, but only brought forth monotheism (التوحيد) and transcendence (التنزيه) and admitted to wrongdoing, he thereby manifested his state and showed his position before his Lord. In this lies the request for deliverance and well-being, so Allah answered him and delivered him from the agony (الغم), which was the distress that had befallen him.

His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (and thus do We deliver the faithful) is a promise of deliverance for whoever among the believers is afflicted with agony and then calls upon his Lord with the like of what Jonah (peace be upon him) called with. His story (peace be upon him) will come in Surah al-Saffat, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَزَكَرِيَّا إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ رَبِّ لا تَذَرْنِي فَرْداً وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْوارِثِينَ** — "And [remember] Zechariah, when he called out to his Lord, ‘My Lord! Do not leave me without an heir, and You are the best of inheritors’" (21:89). This is conjoined to what preceded it, meaning: and remember Zechariah when he called out to his Lord asking for a son. His saying: **رَبِّ لا تَذَرْنِي فَرْداً** (My Lord! Do not leave me alone) is an explanation of his call. The meaning of leaving him "alone" (فرداً) is to be left without a son to inherit from him.

His saying: **وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْوارِثِينَ** (and You are the best of inheritors) is a praise and glorification of Him, the Exalted, according to its wording, and a kind of declaring His transcendence (تنزيه) according to the context. For when he said: "Do not leave me alone," which is an allusion to requesting an inheritor, and Allah, the Glorified, is the One who inherits everything, he declared Him transcendent above having any partner in the meaning of inheritance and raised Him above equality with any other, saying: "and You are the best of inheritors."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ يَحْيى وَأَصْلَحْنا لَهُ زَوْجَهُ** — "So We answered him, and gave him John, and cured his wife for him..." (21:90) etc. The apparent meaning of the speech is that the "curing" (إصلاح) of Zechariah's wife for him means making her young and fertile after she had been barren, as he explicitly stated in his prayer: **وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً** — "and my wife is barren" (19:8).

His saying: **إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَيَدْعُونَنا رَغَباً وَرَهَباً وَكانُوا لَنا خاشِعِينَ** — "Indeed they used to hasten in doing righteous deeds, and they would call Us with hope and fear, and they were humble before Us" (21:90). The apparent context is that the plural pronoun refers to the household of Zechariah. It is as if it is a justification for something estimated and known from the preceding speech. The estimation is something like: "We bestowed favors upon them because they used to hasten in doing righteous deeds."

*Al-Raghab* (الرغب) and *al-Rahab* (الرهب) are verbal nouns like *al-raghbah* and *al-rahbah*, meaning desire (طمع) and fear (خوف). They are specifications (تمييز) if they remain in their verbal noun meaning, and circumstantial qualifiers (حال) if they carry the meaning of the active participle. Humility (الخشوع) is the heart's being affected by the witnessing of greatness and majesty.

The meaning is: We bestowed favors upon them because they used to hasten in righteous deeds and call upon Us out of desire for Our mercy or reward and fear of Our wrath or punishment—or they called upon Us while being desirous and fearful—and they were humble (خاشعين) toward Us in their hearts.

The story of Zechariah and John (peace be upon them) has preceded at the beginning of Surah Maryam.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَها فَنَفَخْنا فِيها مِنْ رُوحِنا وَجَعَلْناها وَابْنَها آيَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ** — "And [remember] her who guarded her chastity, so We breathed into her of Our Spirit, and made her and her son a sign for all the worlds" (21:91). The one who "guarded her chastity" refers to Mary, the daughter of ‘Imran. In this is a praise for her chastity and protection, and a rebuttal of what the Jews accused her of.

His saying: **فَنَفَخْنا فِيها مِنْ رُوحِنا** (so We breathed into her of Our Spirit). The pronoun refers to Mary. The "breathing into her of the Spirit" is an allusion to the fact that the birth of Jesus (peace be upon him) was not based on the usual custom in the formation of a child—which is the formation of the zygote first and then the breathing of the spirit into it. Since there was no formed zygote, nothing remained but the breathing of the spirit into her, which is the Divine Word (الكلمة الإلهية), as He said: **إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was" (3:59); meaning their case is the same in that their creation was independent of the zygote.

His saying: **وَجَعَلْناها وَابْنَها آيَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ** (and made her and her son a sign for all the worlds). He used the singular "sign" (آية), counting them—meaning Mary and Jesus (peace be upon them)—together as a single sign for all the worlds. This is because the sign is the birth itself, which is established by both of them together. Mary has the precedence in establishing this sign, which is why the Exalted said: "and made her and her son a sign," and did not say: "and made her son and her a sign." It is sufficient honor for her that her mention is included among the mention of the prophets (peace be upon them) in His speech, though she was not one of them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Faqih*, Jamil ibn Darraj narrated from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَداوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ ـ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ الْقَوْمِ** — "And David and Solomon, when they gave judgment concerning the tillage, when the sheep of some people strayed into it by night." He said: "They did not [yet] judge; they were only deliberating, and Solomon was given its understanding."

**I say:** What was mentioned in the commentary on the meaning of the verse clarifies the meaning of the hadith.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from some of our companions from al-Mu‘alla Abu ‘Uthman from Abu Basir, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَداوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ الْقَوْمِ**. He said: "Straying (النفش) only occurs at night. It is the duty of the owner of the tillage to guard the tillage by day, and it is not the duty of the owner of the livestock to guard them by day; they only graze (رعاها) by day for their provision, so whatever they damage [then], there is no liability upon them. It is the duty of the owner of the livestock to guard the livestock at night from the tillage of the people; so whatever they damage at night, they [the owners] are liable for it, and that is *al-Nafsh*.

David judged that the one whose crops were affected should receive the bodies of the sheep. Solomon judged for the 'messengers' and the 'three'—which are the milk, the wool, and the offspring in that year."

**I say:** It is also narrated therein, with its chain from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him), and in the hadith: "So David judged with what the prophets (peace be upon them) before him had judged. Then Allah revealed to Solomon (peace be upon him): 'Whichever sheep stray into a crop, the owner of the crop has nothing but what comes out of their bellies.' Thus the sunnah (السنة) proceeded after Solomon, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَكُلًّا آتَيْنا حُكْماً وَعِلْماً** (and to each We gave judgment and knowledge). So each of them judged with the judgment of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with its chain from Abu Basir from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a hadith in which he mentioned: "The tillage was a vineyard into which the sheep strayed." He mentioned the judgment of Solomon and then said: "And this was the judgment of David. He only wanted to make the Children of Israel know that Solomon was his successor after him. They did not differ in the judgment; had their judgment differed, He would have said: 'And We were witnesses to their [dual] judgment' (لحكمهما)."

In *al-Majma‘*: "There was a difference regarding the judgment they gave. It was said: It was a vineyard whose clusters had begun to appear. David judged the sheep for the owner of the vineyard. Solomon said: 'Other than this, O Prophet of Allah, is gentler.' He asked: 'And what is that?' He said: 'You hand over the vineyard to the owner of the sheep so he may tend to it until it returns to how it was, and you hand over the sheep to the owner of the vineyard so he may benefit from them. When the vineyard returns to how it was, each of them returns his property to the other.'" This was narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them).

**I say:** The tillage being a vineyard is also narrated through Sunni paths from ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud. There are other narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) with meanings close to what we have cited. What has passed in the commentary on the meaning of the verse is sufficient to clarify the contents of the narrations.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ عاصِفَةً** (And for Solomon the tempestuous wind), he said: "It blows from every side **إِلى الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها** (to the land which We had blessed)," he said: "To Jerusalem and Sham."

Also therein, with its chain from ‘Abdullah ibn Bukayr and others from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَآتَيْناهُ أَهْلَهُ وَمِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ** (and We gave him his family and the like of them with them), he said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, brought back to life for him his family who were there before the affliction, and He brought back to life for him those who died while he was in the affliction."

Also therein, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً** (And the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage), he says: "From the deeds of his people." **فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ** (thinking that We would not put him to hardship), he says: "He thought that he would not be punished for what he did."

In *al-‘Uyun*, with its chain from Abu al-Salt al-Harawi in the hadith of al-Rida (peace be upon him) with al-Ma'mun regarding the protection (عصمة) of the prophets, he (peace be upon him) said: "As for His saying: **وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ**, the word **فَظَنَّ** (he thought) means he was certain that his provision would not be restricted (لن يضيق عليه رزقه). Do you not hear the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَأَمَّا إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ ـ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ** — 'but when He tries him and clears for him his provision' (89:16), meaning He restricts his provision. Had he thought that Allah had no power over him, he would have disbelieved."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain from al-Zayyat from a man from Karram from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Four for four..." until he said: "And the fourth is for agony and grief: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** (There is no god but You! Immaculate are You! Indeed I have been among the wrongdoers). Allah, the Glorified, said: **فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَنَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ ـ وَكَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (So We answered him and delivered him from the agony, and thus do We deliver the faithful)."

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in *al-Khisal* from him (peace be upon him) as a *mursal* narration.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir extracted from Sa‘d: I heard the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) saying: "The Name of Allah, which if He is called by it He answers, and if He is asked by it He gives, is the supplication of Jonah ibn Amittai." I said: "O Messenger of Allah, is it for Jonah specifically or for the community of Muslims?" He said: "It is for Jonah specifically, and for the believers when they supplicate with it. Have you not heard the saying of Allah: **وَكَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (and thus do We deliver the faithful)? It is a condition from Allah for whoever calls upon Him."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَصْلَحْنا لَهُ زَوْجَهُ** (and cured his wife for him), he said: "She used not to menstruate, then she menstruated."

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain to ‘Ali ibn Ja‘far from his brother Musa ibn Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Desire (الرغبة) is that you face the sky with your palms and face your countenance with them. Fear (الرهبة) is that you cast down your palms and raise them toward the face."

**I say:** The like of it was narrated in *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Abu Ishaq from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "Desire (الرغبة) is that you face the sky with the insides of your palms. Fear (الرهبة) is that you make the backs of your palms toward the sky."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: **يَدْعُونَنا رَغَباً وَرَهَباً** (they would call Us with hope and fear), he said: "Desirous and fearful." Regarding His saying: **الَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَها** (she who guarded her chastity), he said: "Mary, nothing looked upon her." Regarding His saying: **فَنَفَخْنا فِيها مِنْ رُوحِنا** (so We breathed into her of Our Spirit), he said: "A created spirit, meaning from Our command."
